summary: Simon Späti explains why he deliberately avoids traditional research when writing, preferring to draw entirely from personal experience and accumulated knowledge. He views his ongoing note-taking in his 'Second Brain' as a form of passive research that connects ideas over time. With two decades of field experience, he argues that things don't change as drastically as media suggests. His goal as a writer is not to recall information but to spark thinking and inspire readers through authentic, experience-based perspectives.
key_points: Traditional research disrupts the creative writing process and dilutes authentic voice
A Second Brain note-taking system serves as passive, always-on research that connects ideas organically over time
Two decades of hands-on field experience provides more reliable insight than desk research
The goal of technical writing should be to spark ideas and inspire, not merely recall and relay information
Getting hands-on with tools provides a 'feel' that reading about them cannot replicate
money_quotes: The fun of writing is to express myself and explain something in my head. Not researching something and losing focus.
I want to share my opinions.
Things do not drastically change in an instant, even if the media and social media make us believe so.
I see my goal as a writer not to recall info, but to make you think, spark new ideas, inspire at best.
I try the tool and get hands-on. So rather than browsing and reading, I get a feel for it.
I can describe a feeling or experience much better than researching something, and recall them as if they were my words.
key_insight: The most authentic and inspiring technical writing comes from lived experience rather than desk research, because a writer's goal should be to spark new thinking, not to relay information.
